desc:
The road rises slightly towards the east as it approaches a cliff. It appears
not to have been in serious use for a very long time as evidenced by the copious
grass growing up through missing stones. The fact that the road is still
discernable after so long is evidence that it was once a major trade route. To
the west, the road is being undercut by the river. To the south, the Glanduin
continues its trek westward.
name: The Road of the Smiths

desc:
This ancient path leads away to the south and west, hugging the soft, green
banks of the powerful river Glanduin. Although many stones are missing from the
road, the masterful pattern of interlocking cobblestones which remains indicates
that this road was once a major thoroughfare. A tall cliff rises out of the
swaying grass to the east, and grassy plains continue far into the south.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The land here slopes gently southward into the Glanduin Valley - if the word
'gently' can be used of land looking so tired and uninviting. The rocky ground
is covered with clumps of brownish grass and thistles. There are no trees
anywhere in view, nor can many birds or small animals be heard. The landscape
seems dull, drab, and empty.
name: Treeless Slope

desc:
With struggling patches of thistle and coarse grass scattered amongst out-
croppings of rotten limestone and patches of broken shale the rocky ground here
slopes slightly to the south towards the Glanduin. Rising from numerous points
in the cracked bedrock little trickles of water gather together and run down
small channels to form a small stream flowing down to the west.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
These overgrazed hills surround you for miles on every side. Clumps of coarse
brown grass with a hint of green near the roots cling mechanically to life in
the thin clay between the outcroppings of rock. Some larger rocks lie in a line
across a hills flank, too straight to be natural, but otherwise barely
distinguishable from their surroundings.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
Low, stony hillocks covered with sparse clumps of grass extend around you. There
are a few broad-leaved plants around the bases of the larger rocks, but the more
exposed ones have been nibbled down and pawed over. Nettles and thistles grow in
the swales.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
These low and nearly barren hills extend southward to the horizon. Rainfall must
wash off them in an instant, for there is scarcely any soil or humus to hold it
here. Is it possible that great forests grew here once? That is what the stories
say...
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
This is a canyon forty feet deep and eighty feet wide which has been carved by a
meandering stream descending to the west. The water is muddy, shallow, and
easily forded; perhaps in the rainier seasons the flow is stronger. Broad pools
filling the entire canyon are interspersed with long stretches where the stream
is comparatively narrow. Grass, green weeds, and ferns grow at the base of the
canyon walls, and a few hardy shrubs cling to rocky shelves part way up.
name: Muddy Tributary

desc:
These scraggly fields and pastures can barely be enough to support life. The
soil is dried mud; the cattle have eaten whatever forage was here, and have
broken down the rickety fences; the vegetation is rotting and untended. A wooden
palisade about ten feet in height encircles the fields on the south and east
sides.
name: Desolate Farmland

desc:
Here and there the thin soil of this rocky plateau has washed or blown away
completely, exposing a surface of thin-leaved shale which peels apart readily
into grey, sharp-edged flakes of talus. Where there is any topsoil, there is not
enough to support more than a few thorny bushes and some scraggly grass and
nettles. The land seems desolate and empty.
name: Plateau

desc:
The flat ground - composed of sheets of limestone and shale covered with a
paper-thin layer of soil and some scrubby clumps of grass - is broken up by deep
washes running downhill to the north and west. The land is all but desolate. It
does not have an evil feel, but seems very tired and old. In ages past, deep
green forest covered all this land - or so say the wise. Nothing remains of them
or of the men who cut them.
name: Plateau

desc:
Sheets of broken limestone exposed by the sparse soil give this area the look of
an abandoned graveyard. The topsoil looks very thin and worn, as if the land
were tired and drawn out past its time. With little on the ground to hold water
deep washes have been cut running west down the slight slope of the plateau.
name: Plateau

desc:
This is a structure, or group of structures, walled with sheaves of grey-brown
brush and thatched over with mud and canes. The walls, or partitions, are not
solid or built up everywhere as high as the roof; essentially it is one
continuous room broken up into numerous connected passages and cubicles. The
possessions of the dwellers are few and not worth taking; the atmosphere is one
of hunger, poverty, and neglect.
name: In a Hidden Village

desc:
This room, or area, is depressed far below the dwellings north of here; the
earthen walls seem solid, and the air is cool and damp. Torches flicker and
snap. The floor of thin wooden boards is old and cracked, and emits a hollow
sound when trodden on; below it can be heard a lapping and gurgling noise. There
is a fishy smell, and fish scales litter the floor and cling to your feet.
name: Fishing Platform

desc:
This dim, open area is clearly below the surface of the ground; walls of dried
earth enclose it on the east and south. A stone hearth filled with cold, thin
ashes is here; around it are some wicker stools with symbols woven in them, the
only ornamental things you have seen in this part of the village. There is
little to dispel the impression of hunger, apathy, and poverty.
name: Common Area

desc:
Walls of dried and hard-packed earth surround you on three sides. Leaning
against the northern wall is an empty weapons rack, made of rough, unfinished
timbers and wooden pegs. Mouldering, dirty straw gives scant covering to the
bare earthen floor, with rotten scraps of food scattered about near a wooden
table in the centre of the room. To the west the room opens up into a larger
common room.
name: Room of Guarding

desc:
A dozen small bedchambers, the size of storage bins, are connected here by a
narrow passage cluttered by roof-poles and partly obstructed by incomplete
walls. Each has a shabby grass pallet and little else. You see nothing in the
way of clothing, food, or weapons; each villager must be carrying all his or her
possessions around for safekeeping. The outside walls are windowless and the
light is dim.
name: Dwellings in a Hidden Village

desc:
You are floating in a dark, roughly rectangular chamber, apparently under-
ground. The stone walls are completely sheer, and clearly artificial. Water
pours in from a sluice high in one of the walls and crashes down with a
thunderous booming roar. An undertow attempts to tug you downward, but it is not
so strong that you cannot resist it.
name: Flooded Underground Chamber

desc:
A strong current pulls you quickly through an underwater passage. It is pitch
black. The walls are smooth and flat, but you can make out nothing else.
name: Underground Sluice

desc:
Dry grass is shining with its yellow colour all around this area. It is almost
one foot high and grows dense. To the north these plains are surrounded by high
hills. To the east the plains become withered and cracked. The road follows its
course to the south and the west.
name: Plains by the Road

desc:
Scattered grasses struggle to grow here from within the hard packed earth. Large
cracks in the soil caused by wind and rain make life impossible save for a few
hardy species of vegetation. Ants and other small insects roam among the cracked
fissures as if living within miniature valleys. The Eregion plains look
healthier to the west and south. To the north numerous hills forbid passage.
name: Cracked Plains

desc:
A huge granite precipice juts out into the sky here over the cliff face.
Resplendent alabaster columns rise up from the broken stone to defy the wind
that seems ceaselessly to whip out from beneath the cliff. A quiet and cold
power from within the cracked white walls seems to chill the air around the
structure. Standing silent and aged, the temple still seems to be well kept. A
massive exquisitely carved set of marble doors to the north lead within the
temple. To the south a faint trail leads back to the grassy Eregion plains.
name: Entrance to an Ancient Temple

desc:
A long hall leads beneath the towering white marble columns to a stone pedestal
standing at the base of the northern wall. Although kept very clean, the finely
chiselled floor is shattered in places where some ancient tremor forced the
temple to crack. A sacred quiet fills this vast hall and gives the air here an
eerie chill. To the south huge marble doors open upon the Eregion plains. A
small door opens into a private alcove to the west.
name: Alkarambar

desc:
This spartan cubicle is carved out of the western wall of the temple. A narrow
window looks out upon the precipitous drop to the floor of the cliff. A bare
desk and a straw pallet are the only items of note within this tidy white room.
A peaceful aura emanates from patterns of elven runes carved upon the walls.
name: Sanctuary of the High Daedeltir

desc:
Looking westward, the terrain is mainly grassy with the occasional small wood or
hill breaking the fairly flat view. To the north there is a peculiar temple
resting on the edge of a windy cliff. A grassy slope rises up to a hilltop in
the east.
name: Grassy Rise near the Temple

desc:
A black marble monolith has been erected at the top of this hill. It seems to
have some runes printed on it, although the lettering has faded over time. A
marvellous view of the surrounding countryside can be seen from here. To the
north and the east a dark forest covers the land, while to the west and south,
grassy, undulating plains cover the leagues to the horizon.
name: Grassy Hilltop

desc:
Darkness engulfs the tunnel that leads deeper into the hillside to the north. A
slight breeze can be felt in the tunnel, suggesting that it is not just a dead
end. Someone very skilled must have dug this tunnel, for the walls are carefully
hewn and stone pillar supports have been inserted in places. The floor is still
rough, but has been carefully cut to remove most of the rough edges. A dry
wooden ladder leads back up to the surface.
name: Dwarven Tunnel

desc:
Hewn by dwarven hands, this tunnel is a relic from the ancient elven kingdom of
Ost-in-Edhil. A short way ahead the roof of the tunnel has caved in, and no
further progress is possible. The smooth stone pillars look sturdy enough to
hold the tunnel roof, which suggests that they may have been forcibly removed to
seal the tunnel. An old metal plaque stands on a stone pedestal in the centre of
this part of the passage. A cool breeze blowing from the north relieves the
stuffiness of the tunnel.
name: Dwarven Tunnel

desc:
Covered by thick, green grass, this wide plain extends far into the southern
horizon. Not far to the east, a river seems to have cut its way into a rocky
hill, leaving a sharp cliff face in its wake.
name: Wide Grassy Plain

desc:
You stand upon an ancient road, masterfully crafted of interlocking stones which
seem to have withstood the passage of time for countless years. In places the
stones lie broken or missing and weeds shoot up between them, betraying the
mortality of this impressive work. To the northeast a foreboding cliff juts
sharply up from the grassy plains, breaking the monotony of your surroundings.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Cracked stones pave the ancient road that led from Tharbad to Moria. Most of the
stones are missing now, and the ones that are left are cracked and badly worn.
The road follows the cliff to the northeast, with the river just south of the
road.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
As you gaze down through the water, you see myriads of pebbles and perfectly
rounded stones along the bottom. They appear to wave back and forth as the
surface of the water ripples from the current. To the north and east, the road
of the smiths follows the river. The river turns from south to west here, and
the southwest bank is choked thick with holly but gives way to tall grass
further south.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The fast paced current in this section of the river races over boulders and
under logs, making for a dangerous if passable way. On the north bank, you see
the Road of the Smiths, and a steep cliff rising behind it. To the south, the
endless grasses of the Enedwaith strech out before you.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The cold waters of the Glanduin continue on their journey towards the sea,
lapping at the tall, lush grass that grows on its moist banks. To the north and
west an ancient trail follows the river, beyond which lies a steep, formidable
cliff. The faint shadow of mountains can be seen far to the north and east.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The waters of the mighty Glanduin flow briskly northwards, bordered on the south
by some sparse woodlands. To the east a steep cliff rises towards the sky from
the grassy fields, towering over a weathered trail.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
A low cliff borders the river to the north and east, leaving just enough room
for an ancient road to wind its path between these obstacles. To the southeast a
vast, grassy plain spreads out into the distance, with the Glanduin acting as
its northern border. Directly south of here, beyond the river, is a small wood.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
An ancient roadway, its well-crafted paving stones now broken, follows the banks
of the river. A low, rocky cliff prevents any travel to the northeast beyond
this roadway. Shrubs and bushes grows amongst the nooks and cracks of the rocky
cliff face. To the southeast, a vast, grassy plain spreads before you as far as
the eyes can see.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
This ancient roadway leads southeast and northwest from here, obediently
following the banks of the mighty Glanduin. The craftsmanship evident in the
broken stones beneath your feet indicate that this was once a great highway of
some long-lost kingdom. A tall cliff to the northeast blocks any movement in
that direction, and the river makes travel to the southwest awkward without a
boat.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Tall, thick grass grows all about you, towering six feet above the dense turf. A
clump of strange trees stands to the north, while from the east comes the sound
of swiftly running water.
name: Tall Grassy Plains

desc:
Lush, green grass laps at the trunks of the thin, scrawny trees which dot the
vast plain. Under the twisted boughs of these dried out trees, some odd plants
seem to have taken root. Every so often there lies the great charred corpse of
some ancient plant, struck down by lightning years ago.
name: Sparse Woodlands

desc:
Scrawny, weather-beaten trees have clumped together over the years, making
progress through the thick foliage difficult at best. The great expanse of the
Glanduin lies to the north and east, betrayed by the brisk sound which echoes
amidst the twisted tree-tops.
name: Woods by the River

desc:
Waist high grass mixed with tall yellow plumes of goldenrod dominate the
landscape here. To the north, the grass becomes taller and denser, to the south
and west, it just continues without the goldenrods. To the east, some trees and
brush grow thickly along the edge of a large river.
name: Goldenrods

desc:
The grass here is simply too tall to be sure of what lies more than a few feet
in any direction. It seems that nearly anything could hide around here simply by
laying down. To the north, you catch glimpses of a large river, and to the
south, the grass appears to be a bit shorter.
name: Tall Grass

desc:
The grass here is dry and short. Many areas are not even covered with grass at
all, showing the rocky soil and allowing the wind to stir up little clouds of
dust. To the west you see a large finger of rock jutting up out of the plains.
To the east the land is lower and closer to the river with much taller and
greener grass. To the south you see short green grass and flowers as far as the
eye can see.
name: Short Grass

desc:
The grass here is drying out. The tops of most blades are dry and brown, but the
base of each plant is still barely alive. To the east there are trees, also
drying out, but oddly the bushes at their base are bright green with many shiny
leaves. To the south the land rises and dries even further.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The course of this ancient thoroughfare is guided by the sheer cliff that arises
to the southwest and the river Glanduin. Beyond these majestic waters lie plains
of tall grass as far as the eye can see. Looking east, you can see in the
distance some ruins which lie on the northern side of the river.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The ancient paving stones beneath your feet are covered with thick, dark moss,
fed by the moisture of the nearby river Glanduin. Just to the north of the road
lies a steep cliff, covered liberally with cracks that might allow a skilled
climber to scale its considerable height.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
A steep rocky cliff guides the river Glanduin to the west. This ancient road has
been built between the two. Old cracked paving stones indicate that the road was
once a major route from east to west. A dark shadow from the cliff above is cast
across the road here.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Moss and fungus grows amongst the ancient paving stones that form this path,
cast into shadow by the sheer cliff above. Despite the threatening overhang of
this vertical rise, it appears the cliff has stood solid for many years. To the
south the Glanduin flows alongside the ancient roadway, beyond which lies vast
plains of tall grass.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Green moss covers the ancient flagstones that pave the road beneath your feet,
which rumour says once led from Tharbad to the dwarven kingdom of Khazad-dum.
The path is in complete disrepair, yet is still used, judging by the recent wear
and upturned earth around you. Beside the road to the southeast lie some
crumbled ruins.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Some ruined buildings can be seen not far to the south on this ancient road. Its
paving stones are cracked and broken, and obviously misused over the years. The
river runs to the southwest, and a tall cliff makes travel difficult to the
north or east.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Crumbling walls and fallen roofing are all that is left of this once fair
village on the edge of the Glanduin. The stonework looks distinctly dwarven,
although most of it has crumbled past recognition over the many years. A small
path leads to the quay that juts out into the river just to the south of here.
One of the ruins right next to the path looks distinctly like an old inn,
although it is very hard to tell with the dereliction about you. The ancient
road obviously passed through the centre of the village, and over a bridge to
the west of here.
name: Ruined Elven Village

desc:
To the west, a wide flat grassy plain can be seen. The Glanduin runs along the
northern edge, where it has cut its way into the rocky hillside over the years.
In the east, the river disappears around a bend in a rocky valley. The old road
that you stand on follows the edge of the river both east and west.
name: Road of the Smiths at the Entrance to a Valley

desc:
Although the rocky cliff to the northwest is slightly less steep here, the many
bushes and plants covering the slope make it a very dangerous looking climb.
Broken flagstones marking an ancient road run north and west, along the banks of
the Glanduin. Just to the other side of the Glanduin, a steep rocky side of the
valley climbs about fifteen yards, and you can see some woodlands on the top of
the cliff.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Following the course of the Glanduin, the road leads north or south. The river
flows continually southwards towards its eventual destination, the Greyflood
many leagues west of here. This road looks a little overgrown in some parts,
especially where its paving stones have become misplaced or damaged over the
years. Occasional footprints or horse tracks in the mud indicates some people
still use the path.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Shadows from the cliff above cast an eerie feel over this broken road which runs
alongside the Glanduin. Although the cliff is not high, the dense bushes growing
up the side stop all but the most foolhardy from climbing it. On the other side
of the Glanduin, another cliff rises. A rock fall to the east seems to have
blocked the road, but a trail can be seen going over the rock fall.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Boulders dot the area between the cliff and the river Glanduin to the south. The
cliff face has collapsed some time in the past partially covering the road that
leads off to the west. To the east, the road continues up a steep slope.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The road winds along the south and eastern wall of a towering sandstone cliff.
To the south, the road leads down along the fertile riverbed. Tiny rocks
dislodged from the cliff are strewn all across the old road and pieces of brick
have broken away to clutter the surface of this ancient pass.
name: Jagged Bend in the Road of the Smiths

desc:
Between the dense brush to the north and the sandstone cliff to the south, this
broken road seems to run on without end. To the east the road descends sharply
down towards the bank of the Glanduin. To the west it cuts deep into the walls
of the jagged cliff. The bushes to the north seem very thick and almost
impassable.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Dense underbrush blocks any way into the forest to the north of here. Not far to
the south there is a sheer drop, and from the dull roar coming from that
direction a river must run along the base of the cliff. The road looks like it
used to bend slightly to the south here, but a rockslide has terminated it
abruptly. To the west the road continues to run between the forest and the
cliff.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The ancient road continues to cut its way between the cliff to the south and the
dark forest to the north. Grass and weeds grow between the broken stones
littering the ground where the once well-built road must have run, and animal
tracks can be seen imprinted in the earth.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Cutting through the forest, the ancient road is a little overgrown in places. To
the south there is a thin band of trees, and the sound of swiftly running water
can be heard from just behind them. Some of the animals of the forest chirp
eerily, the alien sounds echoing from treetop to treetop. To the north a narrow
trail leads off into the forest.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The sound of running water rises from the gorge to the south of the road here,
while to the north there lies a dense forest of holly bushes. Many old, broken
paving stones indicate that this once major thouroughfare has been neglected for
many years.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Broken stones pave the once great road between Tharbad and Ost-in-Edhil. Many
centuries of neglect have made the road difficult to travel, with lots of loose
or missing stones. The Glanduin follows the line of the road.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The cracked and broken road follows the banks of the Glanduin. The many paving
stones that lie about indicate that this was once a very major route, but it has
obviously fallen into a state of disrepair.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The road winds on to the east and the west following the northern bank of the
River Glanduin. The paving stones are cracked and damaged now, but the road was
once wide enough for two carts to pass side by side with plenty of room for
pedestrians too.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The ancient road bends from west to the south here, following the line of the
Glanduin river. The lands to the east and north appear to have been partitioned
off into fields and farm animals dwell therein.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The road continues to wind its way through the countryside, but parts briefly
from the river here as it turns to the east. A copse of trees forms a buffer
between the river and the road to the south while the river itself continues its
path to the west.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Small stone dykes border the road to the east and north here. Beyond these walls
lie farmlands occupied by farm animals. The road continues to the west and south
while some trees lie to the south-west.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
The ancient road travels steadily onwards to the north and east, tufts of grass
growing between the cracked stones. To the west, a thin copse of trees can be
entered which appears to lead to a ford over the river to the south-west. The
river to the south appears much to deep to be forded, but its bed seems to widen
towards the west.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Twisting around the southerly bend quickly, the river drives ahead to the west.
Even from here, some kind of structures are evident there. They seem to be very
old, but there is little else that can be detected. A rough track of flattened
earth rises to the north.
name: Road of the Smiths

desc:
Balanced precariously near the edge of the cliff is a huge boulder. The only
purpose for the rock must be to block the valley where the Glanduin flows to the
south of here. However, it would take an incredibly strong force to move it.
From the look of the vegetation that grows up to its base, it has stood in the
same spot for many years. Westwards, beyond the cliff edge, only grassy plains
and small hills can be seen.
name: Grassy Plains at the Cliff Top

desc:
The ground can be seen through the tall dry grass, where small animals can be
seen moving about. The plateau begins a descent into a valley to the north and
west. However, the plateau extends far to the east bordered by the valley along
the southern edge.
name: On the Plateau

desc:
A sheer cliff drops down about fifteen yards into the valley to the south where
the river Glanduin flows. To the southwest, grassy plains slope gently up
towards the horizon. A ruined village of some kind can be seen just to the south
of here, bordering the steady waters of the river. A grassy plateau extends
northwards.
name: Atop a Cliff

desc:
The grassy plains extend from far in the north to a point immediately to the
south of here, where there is a steep drop to the river valley below. Over the
edge of the bluff, several buildings can be seen lying next to the Glanduin. A
finger of land juts out into the valley to the east.
name: River Bluff

desc:
An astounding view greets the eye from this high point, high above the river
Glanduin. The river can be seen flowing through a narrow canyon to the north-
east, around the base of this rocky spur and following the cliff edge to the
northwest. Alongside the river there appears to be an old paved trail. Grassy
plains cover the ground between the Glanduin and the horizon.
name: Peninsula Overlooking the Glanduin

desc:
You are on a poorly paved pathway which follows the base of a sheer, formidable
cliff. The handholds which you are forced to rely upon are of questionable
reliability, and the likelihood of a fall is high. Not far above your head lies
a grassy patch of soil, marking the crest of this path.
name: Treacherous Pathway

desc:
The ground is covered with a fine soot. Small fingers of grass have begun to
find their way back to the surface. Several mounds can be seen breaking up the
otherwise monotonously flat surface of the plateau. The burned ground continues
to the north and east. A finger of land juts out into the valley to the south.
name: Plateau

desc:
The tall grass completely covers the ground. Occasionally, movements by small
animals catch your attention. To the south and west the land continues on, but
disappears before it meets the horizon. The plateau also continues to the north,
where a few trees can be seen. The land to the east appears to be completely
covered in black.
name: Middle of Plateau

desc:
The grass thins out here and you can finally see the ground. To the east a large
dark area can be seen. The grassy plateau continues to the south and west. To
the north several trees can be distinguished from the rest of the horizon.
name: Edge of Plateau

desc:
Green grasses grow all over the northern side of this hill. The numerous rabbit
droppings indicate that this is a favourite site for the local rabbit
population. At the top of the hill, a black stone monolith can be seen. To the
north the ground drops off suddenly into a gorge, while to the west a promontory
extends over the gorge, carrying an ancient temple.
name: Northern Slope of a Grassy Hill

desc:
Young saplings grow profusely in the shelter of the hill to the west and the
impenetrable forest stands as a border to the north. The strange lack of older
trees is very disturbing, it is as if no trees had ever grown here until
recently. To the east are the older trees towering together in a choked mass.
name: Wooded Glade

desc:
A forest of massive twisted trees shrouds the earth with a canopy of black
branches. This old forest keeps its world in darkness and forces the plants
below to starve for the warmth of the sun. The silence is uneasily broken by the
sound of the wind swaying through ponderous branches. The forest is impenetrable
to the north but thins out to the west. To the east the forest becomes sick and
unhealthy. From the south can be smelled the scent of dry grass and the Eregion
plains.
name: Stygian Forest

desc:
The verdant forest becomes sick and withered here. A stand of dead trees jut
from the marshy ground like wooden claws reaching from the earth in fury. The
ground is almost entirely made up of black mud with hard little rocks peeking
out. Very sparse grass grows here and bloodsucking insects fly through the air,
hovering over scattered puddles of grimy water. A huge black forest grows to the
west, while the forest to the east looks healthier. A broad plateau stretches
out to the south. Dense clumps of broken trees and rotten vegetation prevent
movement northward.
name: The Sunken Forest

desc:
Trees grow all around you in this leafy forest. Many small patches of the sky
can be seen through the leafy canopy above. Some unusually dark green plants
grow under the protection of the trees, and many small forest creatures can be
heard making noises in the undergrowth all around.
name: Thin Forest

desc:
A large smelly lake breaks the continuity of the forest here. All around the
trees grow up to the lakes edge. Rushes and other water plants grow on the
surface, and many insects buzz all over the wide expanse of water. It is far too
murky to see what grows within the lake. A trail in the forest leads off to the
east.
name: Stagnant Lake in the Forest

desc:
Bushes grow thickly between the dense coverage of leafy branches above you. The
high trees seem to dwarf every living thing, and the thick canopy of leaves
blocks out virtually all the sky. Strange insects can be heard making weird
chirping noises in the undergrowth. From their height and the girth of their
trunks, it looks as if the trees have stood here for centuries.
name: Towering Forest

desc:
Trees tower high above you and their dense foliage blocks out most of the light
entering this part of the forest. Small bushes cover the ground, and they
thicken to the south. The forest is very dry however, and the slightly
decomposing leaves and twigs on the ground may make a comfortable bed.
name: Towering Forest

desc:
Blades of grass can be seen growing up out of the black soot, seemingly left
behind by a recent fire which seems to have burned off the grass from the top of
the plateau. The area scarred by the fire continues to the south and west. The
plains on top of the plateau continue to the east. The way north is tangled with
sickly trees.
name: Plateau

desc:
The charred corpse of a tree, struck by lightning, marks the northwestern edge
of large burned out area of the plateau. The fire travelled far to the south and
east. Young blades of grass can be seen just starting to poke their way through
the surface. To the north is an ancient black forest.
name: Plateau

desc:
The ground is covered with black soot through which some young grass can be seen
poking its way back to the surface. The fire stops at the edge of the cliffs
which abruptly drop off to the south and east. The fire has revealed several
mounds that the tall grass would have hidden. The burned out area stretches far
to the north and east. A road can be seen running alongside the river in the
valley below.
name: Edge of Plateau

desc:
Dense holly bushes grow all about, even overhead. They block out all the light
of day. The occasional tree competes with the holly for space to grow. The twigs
and leaves that cover the ground make it seem very spongy and soft. To the west
there is an occasional glimpse, as of light reflecting on a body of water, while
to the east the trail continues.
name: Dark Holly Forest

desc:
The holly trees surround you almost completely, but yet you can still find a
route through them. The trees have nearly driven all other life away, but still
some other plants cling on tenaciously. Small animals scurry past, out of sight
but not out of earshot. The branches above you interweave to form an
impenetrable cloak from the light above and beyond making the forest a place of
shadow and darkness.
name: Trail through a Forest

desc:
The trail comes to an end here. The bushes and trees simply do not allow any
progress to the north. The trail is visible for a way to the south, but its
route is hardly visible as it wends its way around and under trees. Above you,
the dense boughs of the trees and bushes block out all light, making the forest
extremely dark. Although the trail ends here, you can still push your way to the
east or west if you wish.
name: Dark Holly Forest

desc:
A fallen log partly blocks the trail here, but it can be bypassed without much
trouble. All around, the dead tree's old siblings rise tall and strong. The
forest is thick and healthy, and there is nothing to suggest even that it was
ever not so. The trail runs north and south, as straight as an elven spear.
name: Trail through a Forest

desc:
The trees are dense here but seem to thin out to the south and east. The sky
cannot be seen from here, blocked from view by the tree crowns above. To the
west, the trees form an impenetrable barrier and the branches resist all
attempts at cutting a way through. To the east, the trees thin and grassy fields
can be seen beyond the boundary of the forest. To the south, a road seems to
travel east to west once you leave the forest.
name: Dark Holly Forest

desc:
Holly trees pervade the nearby area having banished most other plants long ago.
The boughs far above block the sky from your view and prevent all light from
entering the forest from above. The density of the trees blocks any possibility
of travelling to your north or east, forcing you to move south or west.
name: Dark Holly Forest

desc:
The grassy field grows right up to the edge of a dense holly forest. Some kind
of animal has grazed here recently, maybe cows or sheep. The occasional dry
stone wall breaks the monotony of the plains to the east. To the south an
ancient road follows the line of the River Glanduin.
name: Fields beside a Forest

desc:
Dry stone walls cover this part of a vast grassy plain that stretches as far as
the eye can see to the north and east. The walls segregate the plain into lots
of smaller areas. Some kind of animal has been kept in these fields, maybe sheep
or cows.
name: Small Fields

desc:
A dark holly forest grows vigorously to the west. However it seems to be kept in
place by some stone walls, and this area seems to be used to graze sheep or
similar kinds of animals. To the east the land rises gently. A few leagues south
a large river crosses the plains.
name: Fields at the Forest Edge

desc:
Grasslands stretch out in all directions. Towards the south, the grass seems to
have been laid out in fields with small stone dykes partitioning off the land.
To the immediate south, a small building can be seen.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The grass underfoot makes for easy travel in this area. The grass grows wild
here and there appears to be little sign of any large animals. The grass
continues for some distance to the south, seemingly partitioned by stone dykes.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This small building has been placed on a small hillock, seemingly to give a view
of the surrounding countryside. It is a simple building and has only minimal
cover against the elements. A small hearth has been built against one wall with
a basic chimney to allow smoke to escape. A small pile of logs lies next to it
along with flint, steel and some tinder.
name: Hut in the Grasslands

desc:
Sheep and goats must have been in this field quite recently, from the look of
the droppings on the ground. Short cropped grass grows all over the ground,
except for some muddy tracks which appear to have been made by the sheep.
name: Grassy Field near a Road

desc:
Moss covered stone walls segregate this great plain into many smaller fields.
The plain rises slightly to the northeast, and a forest covers it to the north-
west. Just to the south is an ancient road, beyond which is a fast flowing river
and then yet more plains.
name: Grassy Fields

desc:
Above your head is a sparse covering of leaves, through which the sky can be
seen in several places. The leaves rustle as the wind blows through them. The
sparse undergrowth here has many small animal trails which zigzag all over the
place. Many small creatures must use these paths regularly. A river flows
westwards just to the north and east of here.
name: Sparsely Wooded Glade

desc:
Dangling from the top of the cliff to the northeast, high above your head, are
thick, stringy roots. Below the sheer vertical rise, the river continues on
towards the northwest. The stone looks very unstable, and there are numerous
chunks of rock near the edge of the river that must have fallen from the cliff.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Running alongside a sheer cliff, the mighty Glanduin continues towards the
northwest in its relentless journey to join the Greyflood in distant lands. A
trail follows the northern bank of the river, sandwiched between the broad
waters and the vertical rock face. Tall grass covers the fields which lead up to
the southern bank of the river.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The river becomes very wide and shallow at this point, making it an ideal place
to cross to the other side. At its deepest, the river comes to about a depth of
waist height. To the south and west, grassy plains cover all of the land, and a
high cliff runs from the south to the northwest, with the river emerging from a
steep valley just to the east of here.
name: Shallow part of the Glanduin

desc:
A sandy beach runs along the inner edge of this bend in the river Glanduin. The
dark, dirty looking sand turns into grassy dunes and then a grassy plain to the
south. The Glanduin flows ever westward bordering the rather uncomfortable, hard
beach to the north and the east. Many small plants and shrubs have found a place
to grow in the sand, but their growth has obviously been hindered by the
infertile soil.
name: Sandy Shore

desc:
To the south and west of you lie vast plains of tall, thick grass. This ocean of
green also extends to the east, beyond which you hear the faint sound of running
water. Far away to the east you can barely make out the outline of some kind of
tower.
name: Wide Grassy Plain

desc:
A strange, ugly looking watchtower stands on a small rise in this part of the
grassy plains. The way it leans slightly eastwards seems to indicate that the
stone construction was hastily erected and it is surprising that the tower is
still standing. At the base of the tower are wide arches, allowing you to see
the marsh on the other side of the tower quite easily. A stone stairway leads
upwards into the belly of the stone beast.
name: Watchtower in the Plains

desc:
Some ruined stone quays jut out into the river as it flows slowly westwards.
Many years of neglect have taken their toll on the old docks that once served
the ruined village on the river bank. The stone is now cracked and crumbling,
which makes the quays look very dangerous to use. Westward the grassy plains
roll as far as the eye can see. The Glanduin is guided along to the west by a
thirty-foot-high rocky cliff at its southern bank.
name: Ancient Quays on the Glanduin

desc:
Lichen and the occasional small plant clings to the rocky cliff that guides the
Glanduin on its trek westward. Although the cliff is not particularly high, it
looks virtually unclimbable. A road follows the river at the base of the cliff,
and some wide plains can be seen to the southwest.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The river bends from the southeast to the west here. An ancient paved road runs
along the base of a cliff just to the north, and some ruins can be seen along
the road to the east. The river seems to widen out to the west.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Creeping forever westwards, the Glanduin flows from the east to the west here.
The river is quite wide and flows quite slowly towards some derelict quays just
visible to the west. The river emerges from the canyon and crosses some wide
grassy plains just beyond the quays. Upriver, the valley twists a little to the
north. To the south the high valley walls rise high above the river.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The river flows steadily to the west alongside a trail at the bottom of a narrow
valley. The steep rocky walls of the valley must have been carved by the river
over thousands of years. The occasional bush or plant can be seen clinging to
the rocks, as well as many mosses. A few small trickles of water can be seen
cascading down from the heights above.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Flowing steadily along the base of a rocky cliff, the Glanduin heads slowly
towards the Greyflood far west of here. The cliff guides the course of the river
from the north and a few leagues south of here, the canyon once again bends
westwards. A black patch half way up the cliff side suggests that there may be a
cave extending into the cliff face.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
A small muddy beach borders the east side of the Glanduin. It seems the river
has dug its way through the hills over the years, leaving a flat bottomed valley
with steep rocky sides. The Glanduin flows from the east to the south here.
There appears to be some kind of road to the west of the river, however as it
follows the bend in the waterway, it gets progressively harder to see.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Widening a little, the river flows from north to west. There is a steep rocky
cliff on the southern side, and some small waterfalls cascade down parts of this
cliff into the river. A dark patch deep in the river at the base of the cliff
seems to warrant investigation. On the northern side is a sandy beach with rocky
cliffs beyond it. None of the cliffs that you can see from here are particularly
high, they do, however, all look very difficult to climb.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Steep rocky walls rise on either side of this natural causeway in which the
river Glanduin flows. The top of the cliffs are not far up but the moss and
lichen growing on the slippery surface of the rocks make the climb look very
dangerous indeed. The river flows ever onwards along the causeway.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The River Glanduin flows swiftly and eternally to the west, passing between
towering clifftops to the north and south. Many small plants and the occasional
straggling holly bush can be seen growing on the sides of the cliffs, but there
is no suitable landing place here, and the only way to go is to follow the
course of the river.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The way guides the river westwards. The steep rocky sides, covered in plant
life, block movement to the north or the south. To the east, the causeway seems
to become far shallower.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Following a narrow causeway to the west, the river Glanduin flows tirelessly
onwards. The rocky wall to the south looks just about climbable, although there
is no way to tell what is at the top of the cliff. On the clifftop to the north
there seems to be the beginnings of a forest, but there is no way up the cliff
at this point.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The river bends a little from the south to the west as it travels through a
rocky gorge. Although the river has descended below the level of the road
already, it should still be possible to scramble up the northern rockface to
reach the ancient path.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Swiftly, the river Glanduin bends to the north to avoid a steep rise in the land
to the west. The river has eroded its own gorge over the years to pass this
shallow ridge. To the northeast a road follows the river, beyond it lies a
grassy plain.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The river is a little bit wider here than it is either to the east or the west,
causing it to flow westwards at a slower pace than the rest of the waterway. A
road to the north follows the river both ways. A sheer cliff-face to the south
prevents you from travelling in that direction, its smooth walls offering no
purchase for a climber.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The cool water of the Glanduin washes up on the sandy river shore. Waterlogged
debris has been thrown aside by the river, and mingles with smooth pebbles and
dark soil. Startlingly green grass sprouts from the fringe. Just beyond the
grass on the north and east bank lie the remains of an ancient road. To the
south-west, the river has cut away at a sheer slope which defies climbing.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
Rocky protrusions break through the grasses that grow on this hilltop, making
travelling somewhat awkward at times. However, this hillside looks far easier to
traverse than some of those further to the west. A road can be seen not far to
the north, but a steep gorge separates these hills from the road. A dull roaring
sound can be heard from within the gorge.
name: Bleak Grassy Hills

desc:
The west bank of the river is very steep and smooth, defying any attempts at
climbing the steep walls. The river begins to travel faster here as it winds its
way through the countryside. You can leave the river to the east where a small
copse of trees can be seen.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The bottom here is too deep to see, but it doesn't help that the water is dark
and cold. Grasses grow all about the river, although far to the south on the
horizon there is a dark line, maybe a forest of some kind. Just to the north-
east a thick copse of trees grows.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The waterway is broad and swift, but not overpoweringly so. The cool river flows
continually west, leading from its origins in the Misty Mountains. Thick and
healthy foliage hangs down from the scrublands to the south and the river bank
gently slopes up to the road just to the north.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The Glanduin continues its swift path through the lands, flanked as ever by the
ancient road to the north. Beyond the road lies grassland with several low stone
dykes marking off fields. The south bank is made up of grasslands with holly
bushes growing at various intervals on the scrub.
name: River Glanduin

desc:
The path slopes gently along the cliff's western wall and nears a summit. Far
below you to the west you can see the River Glanduin easily flowing against the
sheer wall of the cliff. To the east several breaks in the cliff's flank make
travel treacherous. To the north is a promontory which overlooks the Eregion
valley.
name: Ascending the Cliff

desc:
This lofty promontory extends out from the cliff to survey the Eregion valley
beneath. The River Glanduin can be seen extending to the east and west along the
base of the cliff wall far below. Deep within the valley to the west you can see
the remains of a shattered village. Far to the north you can see a green forest
extending along the top of the other side of the valley. To the east across a
small chasm you can see where the cliff continues on. Southward leads you down a
gentle slope towards an area dense with brush.
name: Summit of the Cliff

desc:
A tall column of rock rises here with sheer faces in all directions. Peering
over the edge, you can see the Glanduin River bending round the north and west
while hills form the border to the east and south. From here, you are able to
see for several miles in all directions.
name: A High Clifftop

desc:
Bare craggy hilltops block the view very far in any direction. Sparse grasses
grow on the shallower slopes. Frequent rocky outcroppings break the grassy
areas. Movement is possible to the south and the east.
name: Bleak Hills

desc:
Little is able to grow on these rocky, inhospitable hillsides. Small tufts of
grasses cling tenaciously to any small area of soil that exists with an
occasional stunted bush growing in places. To the south, the hills disappear and
a dense forest grows there, while to the west some breaks in the steep hillside
seem to offer passage in that direction.
name: Bleak Hills

desc:
Treetops cover the land for miles and miles south of this hillside. The forest
starts a short distance down the south facing slope. To the north, the slope
rises towards the sky. In the east, grassy plains cover the area between the
Glanduin and these hills.
name: Hillside above the Forest

desc:
Very little grass grows on this barren hillside. Grey rocks protrude from the
ground making travel difficult. A forest can be seen to the south and a wide
expanse of plains to the southeast.
name: Bleak Hills

desc:
From the top of this hill, yet more hills can be seen all about. Those to the
north and west seem far more barren with many rocky outcrops. Those to the south
and east seem far more verdant, some even completely covered with grass and
holly bushes.
name: Grassy Hilltop

desc:
Gently rolling grasslands cover the ground to the south of here while dense
holly bushes grow to the east. Some large trees seem to form a copse just to the
east. The density of the bushes to the immediate east makes it impossible to
travel in that direction. However, to the north the river Glanduin breaks the
continuity of the plains. There is a road running along the opposite bank of the
river, with plains beyond it. Some rugged hills to the west look difficult to
cross.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Grassy hills rise skywards to the west of this flat grassy field. A fast flowing
river travelling from east to west borders the fields northern edge. To the
south the far edge of the field is hard to make out, but a dim forest seems to
grow at the extent of your vision. Some footprints seem to lead northwards, but
stop abruptly at a particularly dense area of holly bushes.
name: Grassy Field beside the Glanduin

desc:
A ruined wooden hut is perched on the hillside under the shelter of a large
holly bush. It looks as if it was deserted for years, the door swings slowly on
rusty hinges creaking loudly on every swing. Most of the windows are broken and
there are some gaping holes in the roof.
name: Shack on the Hillside

desc:
Tall, dry grasses grow up the sides of this hill, broken occasionally by a
rugged holly bush. Ironically, a holly bush is growing right at the top of the
hill. It looks a little windswept, but it seems to be flourishing. The river
Glanduin can be seen to the north and east.
name: Grassy Hillside

desc:
Here the river runs wide and shallow, allowing passage across the river. To the
north, a small copse of trees obstructs your view of what lies beyond, although
a river seems to travel behind it. To the south lie grasslands, with holly
bushes interspersed over it.
name: Ford of the River Glanduin

desc:
A small copse of trees stands here, forming a small break between the road to
the north and east and the river to the west and south. Short pines stand here
braving the open land here and flourishing against all the odds. Trails criss-
cross the area, made by small game and some larger beings.
name: A Copse of Trees

desc:
Grasslands stretch out to the east and north. You can see that the grass seems
to have been partitioned off into fields by means of some low stone dykes.
Domestic animals roam the fields as well as some smaller wild animals.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Grasslands stretch out in all directions, punctuated by low stone walls. The
fields stretch as far as the eye can see to the north, but beyond some fields to
the south an old, disused road can be seen as it follows the path of the ancient
Glanduin River.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Cows and sheep have been roaming this field quite frequently, making muddy
trails in the gaps between the dry stone walls. A large water trough towards the
centre of the field suggests why they come here so much. The fields slope
upwards to the northeast.
name: Trough in a Grassy Field

desc:
Many tracks criss-cross here, as if it were the scene of much activity. Several
animal trails lead through the mud to the south. You can see a small, low
building to the west, but you cannot make out what may have built or what may
stay there. To the south-west, you can see a road following closely the route of
an old road.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Grasslands continue to the south and west for a long way. Weeds intermingle with
the grass and no signs can be seen of anything larger than a rabbit. Far, far to
the south, a road can just be made out.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Roughly built stone dykes mark the boundaries of fields on these grassy plains.
The tracks in the ground seem to suggest that some animals are kept here by
someone, possibly sheep, judging by the droppings. Most of the tracks seem to
follow an east-west route, but you can see no sign of why. Far to the
south-west, a road can barely be made out, with a river just beyond.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The grasslands here have been partitioned off with rough stone dykes. Evidence
of the fields' inhabitants are obvious as you can see the spoor and tracks all
around you. The fields spread all around, but expand furthest to the south-
west. Beyond the fields, you can barely make out a road following the line of
the ancient Glanduin River. Further to the west, a small building is visible.
name: Grasslands

desc:
This shallow cave seems to be the nesting place for some very large birds. A
large nest takes up most of the space at the back of the cave, while bits of
wood and twigs litter the front part, presumably extra nesting material if it is
needed. The cave looks out over the valley through which the river Glanduin
runs.
name: Cave in the Cliff

desc:
The grasslands end here, but stretch out to the north and east. The south and
west boundaries of the fields here are made by a cracked road as it follows a
river to its south. The evidence of some farm animal, possibly sheep, lies all
around with criss-crossed tracks and spoor.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Bending slightly from the north to the west, the Glanduin flows forever
westwards towards the sea. The sides of the rocky valley rise at least fifteen
yards on either side, allowing just enough room for the road and river on the
floor of the canyon. To the south and east the slope of the canyon is covered
with brush and loose stones that forbid passage.
name: River Glanduin
